file_type: ELF 32-bit LSB shared object, ARM, EABI5 version 1 (SYSV), dynamically linked, no section header
evidence: Winning edge_2 (ssl3_read_bytes): In the record-type dispatch switch statement, case 24 (0x18 = TLS1_RT_HEARTBEAT) calls tls1_process_heartbeat(a1) directly, then clears the record buffer and resets read state. This is the gate that makes heartbeat messages reachable. Additionally, edge_3 (tls1_process_heartbeat) and edge_1 (dtls1_process_heartbeat) show the full implementation: reading a 1-byte heartbeat type (1=request, 2=response), a 2-byte payload length (v10 = v7[2] | (v7[1] << 8)), allocating a response buffer with CRYPTO_malloc, setting response type to 2, memcpy(v13+3, v7+3, v10) echoing the payload, appending RAND_pseudo_bytes padding, and sending via ssl3_write_bytes/dtls1_write_bytes with record type 24. This is the classic RFC 6520 heartbeat extension implementation containing the Heartbleed buffer over-read pattern.

The tls1_process_heartbeat function (edge_3) reads a 2-byte payload length (v9 = v7[2] | (v7[1] << 8)), allocates a response buffer, sets type byte to 2 (response), copies payload via memcpy(v10+3, v7+3, v9) without bounds checking, adds 16 random padding bytes via RAND_pseudo_bytes, and sends via ssl3_write_bytes(a1, 24, v11, v9+19). The DTLS variant dtls1_process_heartbeat (edge_1) shows the same pattern calling dtls1_write_bytes. This is complete RFC 6520 heartbeat extension support including the Heartbleed over-read vulnerability.

Handles heartbeat request (type 1) and response (type 2) with sequence checking. Edge_1 (dtls1_process_heartbeat) shows identical DTLS variant using dtls1_write_bytes. Edge_2 (ssl3_read_bytes) confirms the gate: checks `v51 == 24` (0x18 = TLS1_RT_HEARTBEAT record type) and dispatches to `tls1_process_heartbeat(a1, ...)`. Source file references "t1_lib.c" and "d1_both.c" match OpenSSL heartbeat implementation locations.
